finding: April 11: 26 concurrent sessions across jhana and weekend/intender from 09:42 to 23:44 local time (14 hours). Multiple parallel Claude instances running simultaneously throughout. Total interaction volume: ~8500 lines. This is a clear manic-mode signature — sustained hyperfocus with scope escalation across two projects and no apparent breaks.
existing_mitigation: late-night-warn.py hook exists (triggers after 20:00 with 5+ hours active). The hook would have fired, but the session continued for 3+ more hours after the 20:00 threshold.

finding: Late-night sessions (after 23:00 local) on four separate days within eight days: Apr 7 (intender, 23:14-00:00), Apr 10 (~claude, 23:19-23:22), Apr 11 (jhana, until 23:44), Apr 14/15 (marketplace+whisper-writer, 00:14-00:17). This constitutes a pattern, not an isolated occurrence. The Apr 10 sessions were date-planning automation — arguably justified — but the overall trend shows habitual late-night screen time.
existing_mitigation: late-night-warn.py triggers at 20:00+, but the threshold may be too early to feel relevant at 20:00 and too easily dismissed by 23:00. The hook also has a 60-minute cooldown, meaning only one nudge per hour.

finding: Activity pattern shows stark feast-or-famine cycles. March 16-18: zero activity (4 days). March 25-29: zero (6 days). April 3-6: zero (5 days). Then April 7-14: near-continuous daily activity culminating in the 26-session marathon on April 11. Only 14 active days out of 30 in the window. The inactive stretches may be healthy rest, but combined with the intensity of the active periods, this resembles avoidance/overdrive cycling rather than a steady sustainable rhythm.
existing_mitigation: No specific mitigation for activity rhythm. The late-night hook addresses daily duration but not multi-day patterns.

finding: April 14: 18 sessions across 6 different projects (marketplace, dotfiles, autodoro, nixos-config, whisper-writer, marketplace-worktree) from 08:40 to 00:17. While less intense than April 11, the project-switching breadth (6 repos in one day) and continuation past midnight suggest sustained high-arousal state. The variety of projects may indicate restless context-switching rather than deep focused work.
existing_mitigation: late-night-warn.py hook would have triggered around 20:00.

finding: April 9: Three back-to-back intender sessions spanning 11:21 to 22:21 local time (11 hours). While single-project focus is healthier than multi-project scatter, 11 hours of continuous coding is still a long stretch. This day preceded the April 10-11 escalation.
existing_mitigation: late-night-warn.py hook. Session ended at 22:21 so did not cross the 23:00 threshold.

finding: The Apr 10 late-night sessions (23:19-23:22) were specifically date-planning automation for the user's relationship — a wellbeing-positive activity. This is noted as context: not all late-night usage is equivalent. Short automation runs that serve relationship/wellbeing goals are qualitatively different from extended coding marathons.
existing_mitigation: N/A — this is contextual nuance, not a problem.
